sections.title: A preorganization oriented computational method for de novo design of Kemp elimination enzymes
sections.content:
# A preorganization oriented computational method for de novo design of Kemp elimination enzymes

![](images/6b1d1bb58c3f3b806751c47c0529ff4fad35523cb4d0a9c45f1794ae414e1fcd.jpg)

Shengyu Zhang a,b,1, Jun Zhang b,1, Wenjia Luo c,1, Pengyu Wang b, Yushan Zhu a,*

a College of Life Science and Technology, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China   
$^{b}$ Department of Chemical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China   
$^{c}$ School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, China

sections.title: ABSTRACT
sections.content:
# ABSTRACT

A preorganization oriented computational strategy for de novo enzyme design based on computational enzyme design tool PRODA was developed and demonstrated by the creation of Kemp elimination enzymes. A pre-organized active site model of proton transfer from carbon with a low energy barrier was proposed and then anchored into the scaffold 3AOF, the endoglucanase from Thermotoga maritima, which was selected from the protein structural database. The low-energy amino acid sequences at the binding pocket to stabilize the catalytic productive geometry were computationally generated via the iterative protein redesign and molecular dynamics simulation. The designed variant (3AOF-KE03) bearing 17 mutations was experimentally confirmed to afford catalytic activity $(k_{\mathrm{cat}} / K_{\mathrm{M}} = 14.04\mathrm{M}^{-1}\mathrm{s}^{-1})$ towards Kemp elimination, with measured rate $(k_{\mathrm{cat}} = 0.033\mathrm{s}^{-1})$ enhancement of up to $10^{4}$ -fold. This computational strategy is general, and we anticipate the creation of a wide range of artificial enzymes to catalyze reactions with industrial significance in the future.

sections.title: 1. Introduction
sections.content:
# 1. Introduction

Computational de novo design is a technique to create artificial enzymes to catalyze non-naturally occurring chemical transformations. Typical steps of designing a de novo enzyme include hypothesizing a minimal active site model (Theozyme) and then docking into the existing scaffolds [1]. The binding pockets are then restructured to optimize the interaction between the transition state and host protein [2]. This inside-out computational methodology has been experimentally confirmed to produce de novo protein catalysts for model reactions such as Kemp elimination [3,4], retro-aldol reactions [5], Diels-Alder reactions [6], ester hydrolysis [7], and Morita-Baylis-Hilman reactions [8]. Although the activities of the de novo designed enzymes are generally modest, the in silico designs are highly evolvable and can be optimized to achieve high catalytic efficiencies by either directed evolution technology or computational redesign with ultra-low experimental validation [9-22]. Despite breakthrough progress, the list of reactions catalyzed by computationally designed enzymes remains very limited. De novo design of enzymes to catalyze practical reactions such as triose-phosphate isomerization and Claisen rearrangement has not yet succeeded [23]. All of the chemistry performed by the natural world can

be captured with $\sim 250$ reactions, whereas there are more than 60,000 in the chemistry literature [24]. The future of the synthetic biology where all chemicals are produced in cells by enzymes drives us to develop reliable de novo design algorithms to create enzymes catalyzing reactions not found in nature [25].

The primary obstacle to de novo enzyme design to catalyze practical reactions [26-28] is that the current energy functions and conformational sampling methods do not achieve the sub-Angstrom accuracy needed for efficient catalysis as that demonstrated by Hilvert's work on the evolution of de novo enzymes [9,37]. Moreover, the active site model used for de novo enzyme design always cannot characterize the complicated catalytic geometrical constraints between the transition state and catalytic residues; such defects may lead to the selection of scaffolds with a mismatched electrostatic environment, which cannot guarantee the pre-organization of the de novo designed active sites and will disfavor the catalytic reaction even after sequence optimization at the binding pockets [28]. Therefore, the pre-organized active site models that include multiple catalytic residues and consider the complicated catalytic geometric relationships between the transition state and catalytic residues at the matching stage may help the actual preorganization of the artificial active site and potentially promote the

success rate of de novo enzyme design for practical reactions [29-31]. The recent development of fast and accurate active site-matching algorithms can circumvent the combinatorial explosion difficulties for scaffold selection from the protein structural databases [32]. Moreover, low-energy amino acid sequences in the binding pockets can be searched by deterministic global optimization algorithms to implement the pre-organization of the anchored active site [33], and the catalytic productive binding models can be evaluated using molecular dynamics simulations [4,34-39]. The Kemp elimination (Fig. 1) is a well-studied model for proton transfer from carbon that has served as a benchmark for de novo enzyme design [3,4,40-42]. Here, we developed a reliable computational approach for the de novo design of Kemp elimination enzymes, which combines our computational enzyme design tool PRODA [32,33,43,44] with quantum mechanics calculations and molecular dynamics simulations (Fig. S1). The computational enzyme design program PRODA is available by sending request to the corresponding author of this work.

sections.title: 2. Materials and methods
sections.content: # 2. Materials and methods

sections.title: 2.1. Transition state calculation
sections.content:
# 2.1. Transition state calculation

A quantum mechanical (QM) cluster model (CM) was used to simulate the active centers of the designed enzymes and locate the transition states of the Kemp elimination reaction. Geometries of the active centers are based on the structures designed by PRODA, either before or after a molecular dynamic simulation. The catalytic residues, which directly participate in the catalyzed reaction or bind with the substrate, are included in the CM. The dangling C- and N- terminuses of each residue were capped by methyl groups. An ONIOM model was further utilized to reduce the computational demand. The substrate molecule and sidechains of the catalytic residues were assigned to the high level and described by density functional theory (DFT) method and the unrestricted B3LYP functional with the Ahlrichs def2-TZVP basis set [45] in geometric optimizations and transition state searches. Single point energy calculations with the def2-QZVPD basis set [46] were carried out to determine the electronic energies. The structure of the cluster model can be viewed in Fig. S2. Backbone atoms, including the methyl caps, were assigned to the low level and treated by the semi-empirical unrestricted PM6 method, and were kept fixed during all calculations. Grimme's D3 method was used to describe the van der Waals weak interactions. Transition states were located by using the Berny algorithm as implemented in the Gaussian16 program [47]. The transition states were further confirmed by the existence of a single imaginary vibrational mode along the reaction coordinate. Zero-point energy and entropic corrections were added to the electronic energy in order to estimate the Gibbs free energy changes. Since the Kemp elimination must be catalyzed within a hydrophobic environment, no

implicit solvent model was included. Detailed methods are provided in Supporting Information (SI).

sections.title: 2.2. Scaffold selection
sections.content:
# 2.2. Scaffold selection

A scaffold library was built by selecting appropriate proteins from the structural database PDB based on the criteria provided in SI. For each scaffold, the three largest pockets were identified using the cavity detecting module ProdaPocket in PRODA, with each pocket including up to 100 design positions. A fast and accurate active site algorithm ProdaMatch [32] was used to check whether the active site can be anchored into the scaffold based while satisfying the geometrical constrains. The matches obtained on one scaffold were ranked by the scoring function. Detailed methods are provided in SI.

sections.title: 2.3. Computational enzyme design
sections.content:
# 2.3. Computational enzyme design

The active site design was carried on the scaffold selected from the protein library. Water molecules in the selected scaffold were removed and the amino-acid hydrogen atoms were added using PRODA based on the topology parameters of the all-atom CHARMM 22 force field [48]. The atomic coordinate of 5-Nitrobenzisoxazole was taken from Pubchem, and hydrogen atoms were added using the molecular modeling software Discovery Studio. Multiple sequence selection positions were chosen to vary the amino acid types. Additional positions lying in the vicinity of the sequence selection positions, including catalytic residues were subjected to alter rotameric states. A backbone-independent rotamer library compiled by Xiang and Honig [49] which contains 11, 810 original rotamers, was used to model side-chain conformations of design sites. The crystal conformation of the native amino acid at each design position was also considered as a rotamer. The rotamers for serine, threonine, and tyrosine were expanded as previously described due to the diversity configurations that the hydroxyl hydrogen atom could adopt [29]. A library of tetrahedral TS conformers for 5-Nitrobenzisoxazole was generated and screened using the previously proposed small-molecule placement approach [50,51]. The atomic van der Waals parameters for 5-Nitrobenzisoxazole were obtained from the model molecules of the CHARMM 22 force field, and the atomic partial charges were assigned based on the PARSE models [52]. Free energy of the enzyme-TS complex system was calculated using the energy functions mentioned in our earlier work and sequence selection optimization problems were solved by the previously developed deterministic algorithm. [33,44] To adequately search the conformational and sequence space, a large number of sequences around the global minimum energy sequence were generated by restricting that a predefined number of rotamer types were different for any two sequences. [53] Detailed methods are provided in SI.

![](images/0faba3ffe2cc81fb8d78c08ecd31645e6759fa20e20ad9d4a9270586cc65701f.jpg)  
Fig. 1. Reaction scheme of Kemp elimination. The transition state can be stabilized by the neutralization of the carbon and the dispersion of the generated negative charge by the base, and a hydrogen bond donor can be used to stabilize partial negative charge on the isoxazolic oxygen.

sections.title: 2.4. Molecular dynamics simulation
sections.content:
# 2.4. Molecular dynamics simulation

The MD simulations were carried out with GROMACS 5.0.7 [54] The initial complex coordinates of the designs were copied from the PRODA result. The topologies of designs were generated by GROMACS using the CHARMM36 all-atom force field. while the topologies of the corresponding small molecules, i.e. the transition state, were prepared using the online Cgenff program 1.0.0 [55,56] and its atom types, bond parameters and atomic partial charges were built to be consistent with the definitions of the CHARMM General Force Field 3.0.1. [57,58] The new protein-TS complex structure was immersed in a dodecahedral box whose size was determined by setting the distance between the solute and box to be $10\AA$ , and the box was filled by the addition of about 27, 000 explicit water molecules represented by the water model TIP3P. [59] And then the charge of the system was neutralized by adding an appropriate number of $\mathrm{Na}^+$ or $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ counter ions to the solvent box. Each simulation was initiated with energy minimization (EM) of the system independently using the steepest descent minimization algorithm. Next, both position-restrained simulations which limit the position of heavy atoms on the system (NPT and NVT). Constant temperature was controlled by the velocity rescaling thermostat [60] and constant pressure was controlled by the Berendsen barostat [61,62]. In position-restrained simulations and MD simulations, the LINCS algorithm was used to impose constraints on bonds and angles of the complex, and the Particle-Mesh-Ewald method [63,64] was used to model long-range electrostatic effects and short-range van der Waals interactions were cut off at $12\AA$ , while the differential equations of motion were integrated by a leap-frog algorithm. Finally, the production MD simulation without additional constraints was run for the system. Post-simulation data extraction and analysis were performed using the GROMACS analyzing tools. Detailed methods are provided in SI.

sections.title: 2.5. Structure prediction
sections.content:
# 2.5. Structure prediction

The structures of the de novo designed enzymes were inferred using the protein structure prediction tool AlphaFold [65]. The multiple sequence alignment (MSA) searching was conducted by MMseqs2 [66, 67]. No template was used during the structure prediction. The predictions were run on Colab [68], with each sequence generating five different models provided by AlphaFold. The model ranking was based on predicted Local Distance Difference Test (pLDDT). The predicted structures were constrainedly relaxed to refine the side-chain conformation using OpenMM v7.3.1 with the CHARMM36 force filed [69].

sections.title: 2.6. Chemicals
sections.content:
# 2.6. Chemicals

5-Nitrobenzisoxazole was obtained from Meryer (Shanghai) Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2-Hydroxy-5-nitrobenzonitrile was obtained from Shanghai Bepharm Science&Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

sections.title: 2.7. Gene cloning, plasmid construction, and site-directed mutagenesis
sections.content:
# 2.7. Gene cloning, plasmid construction, and site-directed mutagenesis

The original protein sequence was obtained from the sequence of the selected scaffold. The gene sequence was obtained by reverse transcription of the selected scaffold, in addition with codon optimization by the website (http://www.jcat.de/). The original gene was synthesized by Takara Biotechnology Co. (Dalian, China). The pET28a(+) vector was used to clone the synthesized gene with resistance to kanamycin. The synthesized gene fragment (951 bp) was ligated into the NcoI and XhoI sites on the vector, and the C-terminal hexa-histidine tag was ligated into the wild-type enzyme and its mutants. Mutants were created by site-directed mutagenesis and the mutations were validated by automated DNA sequencing techniques. Detailed methods are provided in SI.

sections.title: 2.8. Enzyme expression and purification
sections.content:
# 2.8. Enzyme expression and purification

The Escherichia coli BL21(DE3) strain was used as the host cells for protein cloning and expression of wild-type and mutant enzymes, and the strains were grown overnight (over $14\mathrm{h}$ ) at $37^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in solid LB medium containing $50~\mu \mathrm{g / mL}$ kanamycin. Single colony growing well was transferred into $100~\mathrm{mL}$ liquid LB medium with $50~\mu \mathrm{g / mL}$ kanamycin, and incubated at $37^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $200~\mathrm{rpm}$ for $7\mathrm{h}$ . Then $5\mathrm{mL}$ liquid bacterial germ each was transferred into $500~\mathrm{mL}\times 2$ fresh liquid LB medium with $50~\mu \mathrm{g / mL}$ kanamycin and incubated at $37^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $200~\mathrm{rpm}$ for $4\mathrm{h}$ . Protein expression was induced with $0.5\mathrm{mM}$ isoproyl- $\beta$ -thio galactopyranoside and cells were harvested after a further incubation at $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $110~\mathrm{rpm}$ for over $14\mathrm{h}$ . After centrifugation, the cell pellet was resuspended in the sodium phosphate buffer $(50\mathrm{mM},\mathrm{pH}7.0)$ and subjected to ultrasonication by $20\mathrm{min}$ cycles with $4\mathrm{s}$ on and $6\mathrm{s}$ off at $60\%$ amplitude. The periplasmic extracts were centrifuged for $10\mathrm{min}$ and the insoluble cell debris was removed. The soluble lysate was purified by Ni-NTA agarose gel chromatography. Subsequently, the fractions containing the purified enzyme were desalted twice to remove the imidazole and NaCl. The purity of the target enzyme was determined by SDS-PAGE. The concentrations of the purified enzymes were determined by the Bradford method [70] using bovine serum albumin as the standard reagent. The absorbance of each enzyme sample after adding the Bradford reagent was measured at $595~\mathrm{nm}$ . Detailed methods are provided in SI.

sections.title: 2.9. Kinetic measurements of Kemp elimination
sections.content:
# 2.9. Kinetic measurements of Kemp elimination

The kinetic parameters were determined by ultraviolet/visible spectroscopic assay. In the experimental group, the reactions were started by adding $200~\mu \mathrm{L}$ of the 5-nitrobenzisoxazole substrate dissolved in methanol to $200~\mu \mathrm{L}$ of enzyme (purified in $50~\mathrm{mM}$ phosphate buffer) in $50~\mathrm{mM}$ phosphate buffer, $100~\mathrm{mM}$ NaCl, containing $10\%$ methanol, $\mathrm{pH}7.0$ . The final enzyme concentrations were $2 - 4~\mu \mathrm{M}$ . The final substrate concentrations were approximately $1\mathrm{mM}$ , $0.75\mathrm{mM}$ , $0.5\mathrm{mM}$ , $0.25\mathrm{mM}$ , and $0.1\mathrm{mM}$ . In the control group, $200~\mu \mathrm{L}$ enzyme was replaced by $200~\mu \mathrm{L}$ of $50~\mathrm{mM}$ phosphate buffer $(\mathrm{pH}7.0)$ . Product formation was monitored at $380~\mathrm{nm}$ in an ultraviolet/visible (UV/Vis) spectrophotometer, in $2\mathrm{mL}$ reaction volumes, at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $5\mathrm{min}$ . The initial reaction rates of enzyme were determined from the difference between the linear slope of the reaction progress curve in the experimental and control groups. The initial rates divided by the enzyme concentration were plotted against substrate concentration, and the kinetic parameters $k_{\mathrm{cat}}$ and $K_{\mathrm{M}}$ were derived by fitting the data to Michaelis-Menten equation. Detailed methods are provided in SI.

sections.title: 3. Results
sections.content: # 3. Results

sections.title: 3.1. Active-site model construction
sections.content:
# 3.1. Active-site model construction

The transition state of Kemp elimination can be stabilized by neutralization of the carbon by a base and dispersion of the generated negative charge; hydrogen bond donors can also be used to stabilize partial negative charge on the isoxazolic oxygen [71,72]. For constructing the pre-organized active site model (Fig. 2 A), deprotonated aspartate was chosen to serve as the general base to abstract the proton, where a serine provides a hydrogen bond to stabilize it. An asparagine forms a hydrogen bond with the isoxazolic oxygen of the substrate, and the main chain amino group of an alanine donates a hydrogen bond to the side-chain carbonyl group of the asparagine for its pre-organization. For the binding of the transition state, a tryptophan was used to offer $\pi$ -stacking and a serine provided a hydrogen bond to the nitro group of the transition state. The ideal pre-organized active site model is inspired by HG3.17 [9], and the catalytic geometric parameters are shown in Table S1.

Catalytic activity of the pre-organized active site was estimated by

Fig. 2. Active site model for Kemp elimination. (A) The pre-organized active site model. The catalytic residues are colored in blue, the residues that stabilize the catalytic residues are colored in pink, and the residues that stabilize the TS are colored in green. The dummy atoms $\mathrm{P_1}$ and $\mathrm{P_2}$ are the centers of the phenyl rings. (B) The transition state structure of the pre-organized active site model predicted by DFT calculations. Orange numbers are key distances in $\mathring{\mathrm{A}}$ , and Mulliken charges of atoms in the reaction center are given in red and blue numbers. Natural population analysis (NPA) was also performed and the NBO charges are shown in Fig. S10A. Imaginary frequency of this TS is $-1028.36\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ .

density functional theory (DFT) calculations at the unrestricted B3LYP/ Def2-TZVP level. This level of DFT has been demonstrated to be accurate in studying the kinetics of enzyme-catalyzed Kemp elimination reactions [73]. The transition state was located (Fig. 2B, Fig. S2) and verified by the existence of a single imaginary mode along the reaction coordinate. The kinetic barrier $(\Delta G^{\ddagger} = 6.80\mathrm{kcal / mol})$ corresponded to a $k_{\mathrm{cat}}$ value of $6.95\times 10^{7}\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ , which suggests that when the functional groups of catalytic residues are arranged in these ideal positions, is capable of achieving high performance. However, this low kinetic barrier can be achieved only if the designed active site is accommodated and perfectly stabilized in a protein scaffold, which is quite challenging and, as described in the subsequent section, the main task of this study. In the finally synthesized enzyme, residues in the active site will inevitably deviate in a certain extent from the hypothetical ideal position, thus the experimentally measured activity should always be lower than what is predicted by the ideal active site model.

sections.title: 3.2. Scaffold selection from structural database
sections.content:
# 3.2. Scaffold selection from structural database

To identify a suitable protein scaffold to accommodate the artificial active site for Kemp elimination, our active site-matching algorithm, ProdaMatch [32], was used to select scaffolds from a scaffold library comprising a total of 17,434 protein entries, which were collected from the Protein Data Bank based on criteria provided in the SI. For each scaffold, the three largest pockets identified by ProdaPocket were chosen as candidates for the placement of the active site, and a three-dimensional grid was generated to accelerate calculation of van der Waals repulsive energy with the protein backbone via trilinear interpolation. A heuristic algorithm was leveraged to search sites in each pocket to accommodate the active site for Kemp elimination according to the catalytic geometrical parameters (SI). Consequently, a total of 105,511 matches were identified in 16,584 unique scaffolds, which spanned extensive protein folds, including TIM barrels, Rossmann folds, jelly rolls, and $\beta$ -propellers. Following the criteria of ranking and winnowing (SI), 15 scaffolds were selected for further analysis (Table S2). The hyperthermophilic endoglucanase Cel5A from Thermotoga maritima (PDB ID Code: 3AOF) [74] was singled out to construct the Kemp eliminase for the following reasons: (i) the overlap among the matched results was moderate, whereas the geometric constraints were well satisfied; (ii) embedding deeply into the barrel provided a highly hydrophobic environment that could enhance the binding affinity toward

the substrate and also increase the $\mathsf{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ of the catalytic base [4,71,72]; and (iii) the TIM barrel can support diverse substrates and chemistry among natural enzymes [75], with plentiful residues that make up the pocket, which can serve as additional binding residues [3]. As shown in Fig. 3A, Asn1, Asp2, Ser3, Ser5, and Trp6 were anchored at His95, Arg51, Thr194, Ala284, and Glu253, respectively. The matched active site contains no native residues, and the binding pocket is separate from the native active site of this scaffold and is deeply embedded (Fig. 3B).

sections.title: 3.3. Active site creation
sections.content:
# 3.3. Active site creation

To stabilize the transition state (TS) and matched catalytic residues, computational sequence design was used to tailor the binding pocket and promote residue-packing around the active site. Based on the placement rules (Table S3) and catalytic geometric constraints (Table S4), a rotamer library with 371 conformers for the TS was generated using the targeted small molecule placement approach developed in our previous work [33]. The initial design scheme is presented in Table S5, where the amino acid residue identities at 13 sequence design positions (L12, N17, H49, V91, N93, H96, E132, N135, I167, H196, Y198, Y250, and W286) varied among 12 amino acids (A, G, I, L, M, F, W, Y, V, C, S, and T). In addition, 5 positions (I54, F130, A136, T170, and W173) and catalytic residues were subjected to side-chain conformational optimization (Fig. S3). Using a deterministic global optimization algorithm in PRODA (SI), 200 sequences were generated and ranked based on the energies of the enzyme-TS complex systems (Fig. S4A, Table S6). The top-ranked 40 sequences based on either total energy or binding energy were further evaluated by multiple short-time MD simulations (SI). Specifically, two 1 ns MD simulations were conducted for each sequence. The 1 ns MD simulation results are presented in Fig. 4A; we noted that the average number of water molecules around the catalytic base (Asp51) were greater than 0.5 for all sequences, indicating that the hydrophobic environment around the carboxylate was destroyed, perhaps resulting in a loss of activity [34]. We observed that a cavity appeared in the vicinity of atom O8 of the TS, which might promote easy access to water molecules. The design scheme in the second round was refined based on the following strategies: first, positions in the first shell and those at which substitutions concentrated on a single amino acid type were mutated to different types. Second, in contrast, for positions in the second shell where multiple amino acid types were selected, substitutions were fixed to statistically dominant

Based on the above strategies, 200 new sequences were generated using PRODA (Fig. S4B, Table S7). Among these sequences, two appeared with the average number of waters around the catalytic base (Asp51) of $< 0.5$ and the frequency of hydrogen bonding between the ND2 atom of Asn95 and the isoxazolic oxygen of TS of $>70\%$ (Fig. 4A). These two sequences were further optimized by introducing single-point mutations in PRODA, and 11 new sequences were produced and evaluated by 5 ns MD six times (SI). A sequence differing from the wild type by 15 mutations, 3AOF-KE01, was selected as the final in silico design of the second round because it showed the highest frequency of hydrogen bonding between the O8 atom of the TS and the ND2 atom of Asn95 as well as the fewest water molecules adjacent to the catalytic base atom OD2 of Asp51 in the 5 ns MD simulations (Fig. 4B). The gene corresponding to the protein structure of 3AOF-KE01 was chemically synthesized and expressed in Escherichia coli (SI). Experimental results for enzyme activity measurement indicated that 3AOF-KE01 afforded detectable catalytic activity toward Kemp elimination, with a catalytic efficiency $k_{\mathrm{cat}} / K_{\mathrm{M}} = 7.626 \, \mathrm{M}^{-1} \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ and turnover number $k_{\mathrm{cat}} = 0.011 \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ (Fig. 5, Table1).

sections.title: 3.4. Active site optimization
sections.content:
# 3.4. Active site optimization

To obtain sequences with higher activity, 20 ns MD simulations were run for 3AOF-KE01 six times. Statistical analysis of the simulation results was collected in Table S9, where the frequencies of hydrogen bonding between the atom O8 of the TS with the atom ND2 of Asn95 and between the nitro group of the TS with the hydroxyl group of Ser194 were maintained only at $55\%$ and $42\%$ , respectively. On average, 0.6 water molecules were predicted to be in the vicinity of the atom OD2 of catalytic base Asp51. Consequently, the TS might be bound in a catalytically unproductive binding conformation, leading to low catalytic activity. One unproductive binding mode of 3AOF-KE01 was shown in Fig. 7A. We hypothesized that such a catalytically unproductive binding mode is caused by insufficient hydrophobic packing inside the TIM barrel. Therefore, 5 positions (L12, H49, V91, I167, and F250) around the hydrophobic core of the TIM barrel were allowed to vary their amino acid types. Mutation of Leu12, which lies at the bottom of the TIM barrel, to Phe (L12F), resulting in a bulky phenyl ring was predicted to decrease the void of the hydrophobic packing and to help prevent the unproductive binding mode of the TS. Other positions were maintained as those in 3AOF-KE01. Based on these strategies, 181 new sequences

(Fig. S4C, Table S8) were generated and evaluated with 1 ns MD simulations. Three sequences with the average number of water molecules around the catalytic base Asp51 less than 0.5 and the frequency of the hydrogen bonding between the atom ND2 of Asn95 and the isoxazolic oxygen of the TS greater than 0.8 were obtained (Fig. 4A). These three sequences were subjected to single-point mutation in PRODA, and 17 new sequences were generated. Among these, 3AOF-KE02 and 3AOF-KE03 were identified through multiple independent 5 ns MD evaluations, of which the average count of water molecules around the catalytic base oxygen were $< 0.2$ , whereas the frequencies of hydrogen bonding between Asn95 and the TS were $> 0.6$ (Fig. 4B).

The 20 ns MD simulations were run for 3AOF-KE02 and 3AOF-KE03 six times, and the results are shown in Table S9. For 3AOF-KE02 and 3AOF-KE03, the contacts between the carboxyl oxygen of the catalytic base Asp51 and the H13 atom of the TS were maintained at a distance of $2.3\AA$ with restrained fluctuation. In only one out of six simulations, solvents were observed around the catalytic base Asp51 for 3AOF-KE03, but solvents were observed in four of the six simulations for 3AOF-KE02. Four bulky hydrophobic residues (L12F, V91M, I167F, and Y250F) of 3AOF-KE03 were compactly packed inside the TIM barrel (Fig. 7 C), which might have contributed to the exclusion of unproductive binding modes in 3AOF-KE01 (Fig. 7 A) and 3AOF-KE02 (Fig. 7B). Therefore, we anticipated that 3AOF-KE03 provides higher catalytic activity toward Kemp elimination.

The experimental results of our de novo designs are presented in Table 1, where 3AOF-KE03 showed the highest catalytic activity $(k_{\mathrm{cat}} / K_{\mathrm{M}} = 14.04\mathrm{M}^{-1}s^{-1}$ $k_{\mathrm{cat}} = 0.033s^{-1}$ ) of Kemp elimination. It should be noted that the saturation was not reached at the highest tested substrate concentration (1 mM) due to the solubility limitation of the substrate [37]. Analysis by circular dichroism spectroscopy (Fig. S5) showed that the secondary structures of the designed enzymes (3AOF-KE03, 3AOF-KE01, 3AOF-KE02) were similar to those of the wild-type scaffold (PDB ID: 3AOF), and they can remain fully folded under optimal conditions of the experimental assay (Fig. S6), thus ensuring that the designed active site was maintained. The wild-type scaffold (PDB ID: 3AOF) did not show any detectable activity toward Kemp elimination (Table 1), and the single mutant 3AOF-KE04 (D51A) based on 3AOF-KE03, where the introduced catalytic base Asp51 was mutated to Ala, lost all Kemp elimination activity entirely (Table 1, Fig. 5). Circular dichroism spectroscopy analysis of 3AOF-KE04 (Fig. S5) indicates that this inactivation was not caused by global unfolding; therefore, our computational design was demonstrated to accurately model the activity of 3AOF-KE03. Quantum chemical calculations can

Fig. 4. Multiple short-time MD-simulation-based evaluation of the calculated sequences. Each symbol represents one computationally designed variant. The abscissa represents the frequency of hydrogen bond formed between the atom O8 of TS and the side-chain amino group of Asn95, where the hydrogen bond angle (Donor-H-Acceptor) is greater than $120^{\circ}$ and the hydrogen bond length (Donor-Acceptor) is less than $3.5\mathring{\mathrm{A}}$ . The ordinate represents the average number of waters within $3.2\mathring{\mathrm{A}}$ from the carboxyl oxygen of catalytic base (Asp51). (A) Two 1 ns MD simulations were conducted for each sequence. (B) Six 5 ns MD simulations were conducted for each sequence. Multiple long-time MD simulation based analysis of design 3AOF-KE03, angle versus distance scatter plots of (C) Asp51-TS contact, and (D) Asn95-TS contact. The catalytic contacts are displayed in the inset. Data points are taken from six 20 ns MD simulation trajectories. The individual distributions are projected onto the axes. The coordinates of the TS geometry designed by PRODA are displayed in filled triangles.

provide more insights on the catalytic activity of our designs. Based on the snapshot at 20 ns of MD simulations of each design, we constructed the cluster models of the active centers and estimated the kinetic barriers. Structures of the transition states and their corresponding barrier values are summarized in Fig. 6. As a comparison, we also included the barrier value of the pre-organized active center (noted as 'Designed'), where each residue is arranged in ideal positions, in Fig. 6D. Computational results correctly predicts that 3AOF_KE01 has the highest barrier because Asn95 is too far from the substrate and the O8 atom in TS is not sufficiently stabilized by hydrogen-bonding (Fig. 6A). On the other hand, the cluster model predicts that 3AOF_KE02 should have lower barrier than 3AOF_KE03 because in 3AOF_KE02 (Fig. 6B), Trp253 lies in parallel with the molecular plane of the substrate and can provide better stabilization of the TS than 3AOF_KE03 (Fig. 6C). Unfortunately, this contradicts with the experimental measurement, which found 3AOF_KE03 to be more active. A possible reason for this inconsistency is that our cluster models are based on a particular snapshot in a long-time MD trajectory, which may not accurately represent the time-averaged structure of the protein. Moreover, in the cluster model we have assumed a hydrophobic environment (no water exists in the vicinity of

Asp51), which may not be true in the experiment if the hydrophobic packing is not perfectly realized. In other words, the cluster models are based on simulated enzyme structures, not experimentally determined ones. The possible difference between simulated and real protein structures limits the accuracy of the kinetic barriers shown in Fig. 6D, which may not strictly correlate with the experimentally measured kcat values. However, the quantum chemical investigations are still extremely valuable because they provide, at the stage of designing, a fast and inexpensive measure to rule out poor designs which, from the quantum chemical perspective, unlikely to achieve high activity, as well as insights on how to further improve the designs. The ability to yield a low kinetic barrier from the cluster model should be regarded as a prerequisite rather than a guarantee for a successful design.

sections.title: 4. Discussion
sections.content:
# 4. Discussion The success of de novo design of Kemp eliminase by PRODA is mainly owing to the implementation of the active site preorganization aided by the computational predictions. The distances between the OD2 atom of Asp51 and H13 atom of the TS (Fig. 4C), and the distances between the ![](images/04e0f7753e747e0d56c732e8f94838cce0c2822bcf3c8313a556f1e227694c45.jpg) ![](images/5106eaba7144c1ec5d5380d734bc6ff214701741c11b0c1166dbfd9eb70506f8.jpg) Fig. 5. (A) Kinetic characterization of designed enzymes. Catalytic activity was measured by monitoring the product formation over time at $1\mathrm{mM}$ substrate concentration. The ordinate represents the product concentration divided by the enzyme concentration that corresponds to the number of substrate turnovers. (B) Michaelis-Menten plots for Kemp elimination of designed enzymes. The reaction velocity $\mathrm{v_0}$ divided by enzyme concentration is plotted on the ordinate and the substrate concentration on the abscissa. ![](images/76fc208b4fd7de4207c4a162c2b4f1d3b299275ea0a0421d81dae65f6745194b.jpg) ![](images/fc767fe8017c12b0176b5b76749139b1af1399ef6a24f8eeb6e2fcaacd957aaf.jpg) ![](images/43f08fedbbad429a95e06ef0697f87df28866e7d8d70620652d29fbebeb2b146.jpg) ![](images/ca96dde2ee14b149634bb844b28b72fa46806bde787b775c5bde7842721bece6.jpg) Fig. 6. Transition state structures of 3AOF_KE01 (A), 3AOF_KE02 (B), and 3AOF_KE03 (C) predicted by DFT calculations. Geometries of the active centers are based on the structures after 20 ns MD simulation. Orange numbers are key distances in Å, and Mulliken charges of atoms in the reaction center are given in red and blue numbers. Imaginary frequencies associated with TS structures shown in (A), (B), and (C) are $-1,013.90\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ , $-1,040.49\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ , and $-904.63\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ , respectively. NBO charges for structure (A)-(C) are further given in Fig. S10(C)-(D). (D) Free energy profile of Kemp elimination reaction catalyzed by four of our designs. HD22 atom of Asn95 and the O8 atom of the TS (Fig. 4D) are shorter than $3.0\AA$ in most snapshots during 20 ns MD simulation of 3AOF-KE03. The catalytic residues in this model (Fig. 2A) have all been confirmed in the pre-organized active site by MD simulation, except for Asn95 (H95N) whose side-chain carbonyl group lost its hydrogen bond with the backbone amino group of Ile51 owing to the change in the Table 1 Kinetic parameters of the wild-type and variants for cleavage of 5- nitrobenzisoxazole. ${}^{\mathrm{a}}$ .

Reaction conditions: $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $50\mathrm{mM}$ sodium phosphate buffer $\mathrm{pH}7.0$ , $100\mathrm{mM}$ NaCl, $10\%$ MeOH. b Rate accelerations for the Kemp elimination catalysts were calculated based on the rate constant for the uncatalyzed reaction determined in ref 3. $(k_{\mathrm{uncat}} = 1.16 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~s}^{-1})$ . backbone conformation from a twisted $\beta$ -strand to a parallel $\beta$ -strand; this carbonyl oxygen formed a new hydrogen bond with the hydroxyl group of Ser93 (A93S) to stabilize the hydrogen bond formed between the side-chain amino group of Asn95 (H95N) and the isoxazolic oxygen of the TS. Moreover, the hydrophobic environment at the active site was successfully realized which was needed for the Kemp elimination. It should be noted that the pre-organized active site model for Kemp elimination constructed in this paper was attributed to HG3.17 [9], which was acquired after 17 rounds of directed evolution based on the computational de novo design. In the absence of any previous design or natural counterparts, our computational protocol for constructing de novo active site starts to choose a catalytic mechanism for the target reaction and then to use quantum mechanical calculations to create an idealized active site with protein functional groups positioned so as to maximize the transition state stabilization [3]. The pre-organized active site model generally requires multiple residues to represent the complicated catalytic geometric relationships, which will inevitably lead to exponential combinations for scaffold selection from the protein structural database. Fortunately, our fast and accurate active site-matching algorithm could overcome this obstacle [32] and rapidly screened the protein structural database to identify suitable scaffolds to anchor the pre-organized active site model. The location of global minimum energy conformation sequences ![](images/dcf49db0db5c4b9a56222bf5eb696f569195fffa481c39fecb253c6760284076.jpg) ![](images/68b54da900e9f2c374ecd98a9f688d9a53f251094023a05fa139eeb7fb82a657.jpg) ![](images/f71588aa2ab4231a895156637529c7a1d2a84aa3fb4013c286c959d3d30464e0.jpg) ![](images/490786bd4f1d63f83e00f520f8016e52c0bf73d1b8de7c572254159ee207d335.jpg) Fig. 7. The unproductive binding modes of 3AOF-KE01 (A) and 3AOF-KE02 (B), productive binding mode of 3AOF-KE03 (C) predicted by 20 ns MD simulations, and designed binding mode of 3AOF-KE03 (D). The protein secondary structure is shown in silver ribbon. The substrate is shown in ball-and-stick model, the catalytic residues are shown in stick model, and residues at position 12, 91, 167, 250 are shown in ball model, where the added four mutations in 3AOF-KE03 are anticipated to exclude the unproductive binding modes observed in 3AOF-KE01 and 3AOF-KE02. Atoms O, N and S are colored in red, teal, and orange, respectively. The hydrogen bonds are shown in dotted green lines, and the $\pi-\pi$ interactions are shown in dotted pink lines. facilitated the preorganization of the catalytic residues in the active site model. Using PRODA, the deterministic optimization algorithm based on dead-end elimination, linear programming, and mixed integer linear programming guaranteed the identification of high-quality sequences with low energy of the complex enzyme-TS system [33,44]. The assumptions in modeling methods of PRODA for protein-ligand interaction, including a rigid backbone, implicit solvent, and discrete side-chain conformations, can lead to calculation results divergent from the observed structure. Conversely, the explicit solvent model and continuous conformation change are adopted in the MD simulations to complement the energy-based optimization method. Therefore, the high-throughput MD simulation-based evaluation of the calculated sequences used in this research can effectively eliminate the false-positive results predicted by PRODA. The existence of multiple minima on the binding energy landscape can lead to inactive binding modes [39]. The computational strategy of PRODA for enzyme design, which focuses on target binding conformation, can produce sequences with multiple neighboring energy minima which might dominate the designed structure and inactivate the enzyme [34]. MD simulation-based evaluation can discover catalytically unproductive binding modes [4,34,35]. We used this strategy for 3AOF-KE01 to eliminate unproductive binding modes, resulting in the creation of 3AOF-KE03, which increased the turnover number from $0.011\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ to $0.033\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ . Although the turnover number of 3AOF-KE03 $(0.033\mathrm{s}^{-1})$ is well below those of the computationally designed Kemp eliminases KE59 $(0.29\mathrm{s}^{-1})$ and HG-3 $(0.68\mathrm{s}^{-1})$ , it is comparable to those of KE07 $(0.018\mathrm{s}^{-1})$ and 1A53-2 $(0.09\mathrm{s}^{-1})$ [3,4]. Therefore, the iterative design strategy combining energy optimization and MD simulation rapidly locates active sequences with productive binding modes from a huge sequence space. It should be noted that several groups including Head-Gordon, Houk, and Janssen have proposed and applied the molecular dynamics simulation to evaluate and assess the de novo designed enzymes since their creation [4, 18, 32-35]. The main contribution of this research is to use molecular dynamics simulation iteratively to select and optimize the sequences with catalytically binding energy landscapes. The introduction of multi-point mutations might make a difference to the original protein backbone structure, in which case the activity of the initial design can be further improved by an additional round of design based on the crystal structure after the change [27]. To verify whether the scaffolds have been altered in this research, AlphaFold [65] was used to predict structures of our designs and compared them with the starting backbone 3AOF. As shown in Fig. 8A, the root means square deviation of backbone atoms between 3AOF and predicted 3AOF_KE03 is $0.54\AA$ , indicating that there is no significant variation in the scaffold. For each introduced catalytic residues except Ser194, there is only a slight change in the position of CA and CB atoms, while the side-chain orientations of Asp51 and Asn95 are the opposite to the intended design, which is consistent with the prediction of MD simulations (Fig. S9). As the accuracy of the breakthrough protein structure prediction tool AlphaFold has been demonstrated to be competitive with the experiment in a majority of cases, Fig. 8 verifies that our computational enzyme design predictions are accurate. According to the pLDDT confidence measure output by Alphafold, the per-residue accuracy of the structure can be reliably inferred. Compared with 3AOF (Fig. 8B), the per-residue pLDDT scores of 3AOF_KE03 (Fig. 8C) around the active site are similar to those in the native scaffold, but scores around L12F/N17V and Y250F/E253W decrease significantly, while scores around V91M/N93S/H95N/H96W and E132L/N135A drop slightly. The per-residue pLDDT scores of 3AOF_KE01 (Fig. S8A) around Leu12 are almost identical to those in ![](images/b657932542b6c8d2092d2f078af639baae0112bd7823a220f4577726dad36682.jpg) A ![](images/dedaad704455308eb42ce2cdf3588b17ca2576851f9f5717e2f0c0c722fadeab.jpg) B ![](images/2a9ec4e128829877768f64df46cd54782276d98fa00284e53344c49fbcdf8f1b.jpg) C Fig. 8. Results of structure prediction. (A) The aligned backbone structure of 3AOF (green) and 3AOF_KE03 (yellow, predicted by Alphafold). (B) The per-residue pLDDT of 3AOF. (C) The per-residue pLDDT of 3AOF_KE03. 3AOF (Fig. 8B), while they drop sharply in 3AOF_KE02 (Fig. S8B) and 3AOF_KE03 (Fig. 8C) who mutate Leu12 to Phe, suggesting that while functioned as a steric hindrance, L12F might be infeasible in that position and would destabilize the scaffold. Thus, to improve the activity of 3AOF_KE03, further refinement in the pre-organization of catalytic residues is required, while more residues around L12F should be redesigned to mitigate the negative effect of mutating Leu to Phe.

sections.title: 5. Conclusion
sections.content:
# 5. Conclusion

In summary, a pre-organization oriented computational design approach was developed to produce a de novo active site for Kemp elimination in an inert scaffold (PDB ID: 3AOF), which was selected from the protein structural database. The de novo designed enzyme 3AOF-KE03 was created by simultaneously introducing 17 mutations and provided a catalytic efficiency of $k_{\mathrm{cat}} / K_{\mathrm{M}} = 14.04 \, \mathrm{M}^{-1} \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ , which reached the level of KE07 designed by Röthlisberger et al. ( $k_{\mathrm{cat}} / K_{\mathrm{M}} = 12.2 \, \mathrm{M}^{-1} \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ ) [3]. It should be noted that very limited experimental tests have successfully led to the invention of de novo designed enzymes for Kemp elimination, indicating that our computational approach is reliable for producing starting activity for new reactions. The computational enzyme design strategy developed in this research is general, and we anticipate the creation of artificial enzymes for practical reactions with high catalytic activities in the future.
